title: The Competency Commission: How the Federal Government Transferred 90 Million Acres of Native American Land Through Paperwork Recorded as Lawful Commerce.
teaser: In 1887 Native American tribes held 138 million acres. By 1934 they held 48 million. The difference is 90 million acres. The mechanism was not primarily violence. It was a federal form, filed in a ledger, archived in Record Group 75 at the National Archives. The paperwork has been there the entire time.
seriesThesis: The most effective instruments of population devastation in American history did not leave bodies. They left paperwork. ClownBinge has reviewed the paperwork.
seriesConnectiveTissue: Three populations. Three legal mechanisms. One documented operational pattern: legal recognition, secondary nullification procedure, asset transfer recorded as legitimate commerce. The Dawes Act competency commissions. The California Land Act courts. The Tulsa riot exclusion clauses. The archive holds all of it.
seriesNote: REPLIT: Render a series banner at the top of this article displaying the series name, series thesis, and links to ITP-2 and ITP-3. This banner should appear on all three ITP articles identically.

body:
Competent is a legal term. In the United States between 1887 and 1934, it was also a confiscation mechanism. The federal government, through the Office of Indian Affairs, dispatched field commissions across Native American territory with authority to evaluate individual tribal members and render a formal legal determination: competent or incompetent to manage their own affairs. The determination was not a medical finding. It was not a judicial proceeding with counsel, evidence standards, or appeal rights. It was an administrative decision made by federal employees, recorded on a government form, and filed in a ledger. The consequences were the transfer of land. The paperwork is in the National Archives, Record Group 75, Records of the Bureau of Indian Affairs. It is organized, catalogued, and open to the public. It has been there the entire time.

The mechanism that made the competency commission necessary was the Dawes Act of 1887, formally the General Allotment Act, passed by the 50th Congress and signed into law on February 8, 1887. The Act's stated purpose, documented in its Congressional debate record, was the assimilation and civilization of Native American peoples through the conferral of individual land ownership. The logic presented to Congress was that communal tribal land ownership prevented Native Americans from developing the habits of industry and self-reliance that individual property ownership would produce. The Act authorized the President to survey Native American tribal lands and divide them into individual allotments: 160 acres for heads of families, 80 acres for single adults. Each allottee would receive a trust patent, meaning the federal government held the title in trust for 25 years during which the land could not be sold or taxed. After 25 years, the allottee would receive full citizenship and fee simple title. The land not allotted to individual tribal members was declared surplus and opened to white settlement and purchase. In 1887, Native American tribes held approximately 138 million acres. The arithmetic of what followed is in the federal record.

The allotment mechanism worked in three documented steps. First, a federal surveyor mapped the tribal territory and calculated the acreage required for individual allotments based on the enrolled tribal population. Second, allotments were assigned to individual tribal members from the tribal rolls maintained by the Office of Indian Affairs. Third, the remaining acreage, everything not assigned as an individual allotment, was declared surplus and transferred to federal ownership for sale to white settlers at $1.25 per acre. The surplus calculation was not incidental to the Act. It was its primary financial engine. The more tribal members who could be excluded from the rolls, the more surplus acreage was generated. The fewer allotments assigned, the more land became available for white purchase. The Office of Indian Affairs maintained the rolls. The Office of Indian Affairs conducted the surveys. The Office of Indian Affairs determined what constituted surplus. The institutional conflict of interest is documented in the agency's own records at the National Archives. ClownBinge has reviewed the record group index. The documents are there.

The Burke Act of 1906 added the competency commission to this architecture and in doing so created the mechanism that accelerated the dispossession beyond what the Dawes Act's 25-year trust period had managed to accomplish. The Burke Act, sponsored by Representative Charles Burke of South Dakota and signed into law on May 8, 1906, amended the Dawes Act in one specific and consequential way: it gave the Secretary of the Interior authority to declare individual allottees competent to manage their own affairs before the 25-year trust period expired. A declaration of competency immediately converted the allottee's trust patent to fee simple title. Fee simple title meant the land could be sold. It also meant the land was immediately subject to state and county property taxes. The competency commission was the field instrument of this authority. Teams of federal officials traveled to allotted territories, interviewed individual Native Americans, assessed their English language ability, their farming practices, their financial literacy as determined by the commission members, and rendered a competency finding. The finding was recorded on a form. The form went to Washington. The Secretary issued the declaration. The trust patent converted to fee simple. The tax bill arrived. The land went on the market.

The documented outcomes of competency declarations follow a pattern that the Meriam Report of 1928 would later quantify with federal precision. Native Americans declared competent by the commissions frequently lacked the financial resources to pay the property taxes that immediately attached to their newly fee-simple land. County tax authorities placed liens on the properties. When taxes went unpaid, the counties sold the land at tax auction. The auction prices documented in county records across Oklahoma, South Dakota, and other allotted territories show land selling for fractions of its assessed value, sometimes for the amount of the tax debt alone. The purchasers at those auctions are documented in the same county deed records. They were frequently white businessmen, land speculators, and in documented cases, the same individuals who had served on or had relationships with the competency commissions that generated the declarations. ClownBinge makes no assertion of coordination. The deed records and the commission records are separate documents in separate archives. They are also public records that any researcher can examine simultaneously. The dates on the competency declarations and the dates on the subsequent tax sale deeds are in the documents.

The acreage arithmetic is the most precise receipt in the record. In 1887, when the Dawes Act was signed, Native American tribes held approximately 138 million acres of land in the United States, documented in the Office of Indian Affairs annual reports to Congress for that year. By 1934, when the Indian Reorganization Act reversed the allotment policy, Native American land holdings had been reduced to approximately 48 million acres, documented in the same annual report series. The difference is 90 million acres. Ninety million acres transferred from Native American ownership to white ownership through the documented legal mechanisms of the Dawes Act, the Burke Act competency commissions, the surplus land declarations, and the tax sale proceedings in county courthouses across the United States. The transfer was not accomplished by a single dramatic act. It was accomplished by 47 years of paperwork, filed annually, recorded in ledgers, archived in Record Group 75 at the National Archives, and available to any citizen who requests the documents. The 90 million acres did not disappear. They were recorded as transferred. The recording is the receipt.

The surplus land mechanism deserves specific documentation because it is the component of the allotment system most thoroughly elided from standard historical coverage. When a tribal territory was surveyed for allotment, the calculation proceeded as follows: tribal enrollment rolls, maintained by the Office of Indian Affairs and frequently contested by tribal members who were excluded, determined how many allotments would be assigned. The acreage of those allotments was subtracted from the total tribal territory. The remainder was surplus. In the Cherokee Outlet alone, a territory of approximately 6.5 million acres in present-day Oklahoma, the surplus land declaration in 1893 generated what the federal government organized as the Cherokee Strip Land Run, in which approximately 100,000 white settlers raced to claim homesteads on land that had been Cherokee territory under the Treaty of New Echota. The treaty is a public document. The surplus declaration is a public document. The land run is documented in the Oklahoma Historical Society archives. The sequence of those three documents, treaty, surplus declaration, land run, is the operational pattern of the Ivory Terror Project stated in three filing dates.

The Meriam Report of 1928 is the receipt that collapses every possible defense of the allotment system because it is the federal government's own documented finding. Formally titled The Problem of Indian Administration, the report was commissioned by the Department of the Interior and conducted by the Institute for Government Research, later known as the Brookings Institution. It was published in February 1928 after an 18-month investigation by a team of researchers who visited 95 reservations, schools, and hospitals across the United States. The report's findings on allotment are not ambiguous. It documented that the allotment policy had resulted in the loss of the best Native American agricultural land to white ownership, that allottees declared competent had been systematically dispossessed through tax sales and fraudulent transactions, that the Office of Indian Affairs had failed to protect the interests of the people it was legally mandated to serve, and that the economic condition of Native Americans under the allotment system was one of poverty and landlessness. The report was submitted to the Secretary of the Interior in 1928. The allotment policy continued for six more years. The Indian Reorganization Act did not pass until 1934. The Meriam Report is a public document available through the National Archives and the Brookings Institution. The six-year gap between the government's own documented finding and the policy change is also in the record.

The treaty record is the prior legal recognition that the allotment system's architects had to navigate around rather than through. The Treaty of Fort Laramie of 1868, ratified by the United States Senate and signed by President Ulysses S. Grant, recognized the Great Sioux Reservation as the permanent and exclusive territory of the Lakota Nation, encompassing approximately 60 million acres including the Black Hills. The treaty text, available through the National Archives and the Avalon Project at Yale Law School, contains specific language: the United States agrees that no persons shall ever be permitted to pass over, settle upon, or reside in the territory described. The Black Hills gold rush of 1874 produced pressure for access to the territory. The federal government's documented response was not to honor the treaty. It was to negotiate a new agreement. When negotiations failed to produce a cession at acceptable terms, Congress passed the Agreement of 1877, which transferred the Black Hills from Lakota to federal ownership without the consent of three-fourths of adult male tribal members required by the 1868 treaty's own amendment procedure. The United States Supreme Court ruled in United States v. Sioux Nation of Indians in 1980 that the 1877 taking was a violation of the Fifth Amendment's takings clause and awarded $17.5 million plus interest, totaling approximately $106 million by 1980. The Lakota Nation has refused the payment every year since. The money, now exceeding $1 billion in a federal escrow account, remains unclaimed. The Lakota position is that the Black Hills are not for sale. The Supreme Court opinion, the treaty text, and the escrow account balance are all public documents.

The population arithmetic completes the documented picture that the land arithmetic begins. Pre-contact Native American population estimates vary by methodology and source, with the documented scholarly range running from 2 million to 18 million people north of the Rio Grande at the time of European contact in the late fifteenth century. The United States Census of 1900 counted 237,196 Native Americans in the continental United States. The causes of that population collapse are documented across multiple categories in the historical record: epidemic disease to which Native populations had no prior immunity, direct military campaigns documented in War Department records, forced displacement documented in Bureau of Indian Affairs records, deliberate destruction of food sources documented in Army field reports, and the documented conditions of poverty, malnutrition, and inadequate medical care on reservations documented in the Meriam Report and its predecessor investigations. The population collapse and the land transfer are not two separate histories. They are the same history documented in different record groups at the National Archives. The people who held the 138 million acres in 1887 were the survivors and descendants of a population that had been reduced by between 87 and 99 percent depending on the pre-contact estimate used. The allotment system was applied to what remained.

The self-concealing architecture of the allotment system is its most consequential feature and the one most thoroughly absent from standard historical coverage. Every transaction in the system was recorded as a lawful legal proceeding. The competency declaration was a valid administrative determination by authorized federal officials. The tax sale was a valid exercise of county taxing authority. The surplus land purchase was a valid homestead or cash transaction under federal land law. The deed that transferred 160 acres of former Muscogee territory from a Native American allottee to a white Oklahoma land speculator in 1908 reads, on its face, as an ordinary real estate transaction. The grantor conveys to the grantee for consideration received. The consideration is recorded. The signatures are witnessed. The document is filed in the county courthouse. Nothing in the document says what it is. What it is, is the terminal step in a bureaucratic process that began with a federal surveyor, proceeded through an enrollment commission, continued through a competency determination, and concluded with a tax bill that the grantor could not pay. The deed records do not contain that history. The history is in five separate record groups across three different archives. ClownBinge has identified the record groups. The documents are open.

The Indian Reorganization Act of 1934, signed by President Franklin Roosevelt on June 18, 1934, ended the allotment policy and is the federal government's own documented acknowledgment that the system had failed on its stated terms. The Act prohibited further allotment of tribal lands, extended the trust period on existing allotments indefinitely, and authorized the Secretary of the Interior to restore surplus lands to tribal ownership where possible. The restoration provision is the receipt. You cannot restore land to tribal ownership unless you have documented that the land was removed from tribal ownership through a process that the federal government now recognized as requiring correction. The Act did not use the word confiscation. It did not use the word fraud. It used the word restoration. The distinction in terminology is noted. The operational meaning is in the record. Between 1887 and 1934, 90 million acres were transferred. After 1934, approximately 7 million acres were eventually restored through the Act's provisions. The arithmetic of what was not restored is also in the federal record.

The Ivory Terror Project is not an argument about malice. The primary source record does not establish what federal officials intended in their private thoughts. It establishes what they did in their official capacity, what they recorded in government documents, and what the documented outcomes were across 47 years of administered policy. The competency commissions filed their determinations. The county courthouses recorded the tax sales. The Office of Indian Affairs published its annual reports to Congress. The Meriam Report documented the results. The Indian Reorganization Act acknowledged that restoration was necessary. All of it is in the archive. None of it requires interpretation. It requires only reading the documents in the sequence in which they were created and noting what each one records.

The paperwork is still in the archive. It has always been there.

primarySources.citation: U.S. Congress. (1887). General Allotment Act (Dawes Act), 24 Stat. 388. 50th Congress, Session 1. U.S. Government Publishing Office, Washington, D.C.

primarySources.title: Burke Act of 1906, 59th Congress
primarySources.citation: U.S. Congress. (1906). Burke Act, 34 Stat. 182. 59th Congress, Session 1. U.S. Government Publishing Office, Washington, D.C. Amending the Dawes Act to authorize competency determinations by the Secretary of the Interior.

primarySources.title: National Archives: Records of the Bureau of Indian Affairs, Record Group 75
primarySources.citation: U.S. National Archives and Records Administration. (1824-1990). Records of the Bureau of Indian Affairs. Record Group 75. National Archives, Washington, D.C. Contains competency commission records, allotment schedules, enrollment rolls, and annual reports to Congress.

primarySources.title: Meriam Report: The Problem of Indian Administration, 1928
primarySources.citation: Meriam, L., et al. (1928). The Problem of Indian Administration. Institute for Government Research (Brookings Institution), Baltimore, MD: Johns Hopkins Press. Federal investigation documenting systematic failure of allotment policy and dispossession of Native American allottees.

primarySources.title: Indian Reorganization Act of 1934 (Wheeler-Howard Act)
primarySources.citation: U.S. Congress. (1934). Indian Reorganization Act, 48 Stat. 984. 73rd Congress, Session 2. U.S. Government Publishing Office, Washington, D.C. Ending allotment policy and authorizing restoration of surplus lands to tribal ownership.

primarySources.title: Treaty of Fort Laramie, 1868
primarySources.citation: U.S. Government and Lakota Nation. (1868). Treaty of Fort Laramie, 15 Stat. 635. Ratified by U.S. Senate, February 16, 1869. Recognizing the Great Sioux Reservation as permanent and exclusive Lakota territory encompassing approximately 60 million acres.

primarySources.title: United States v. Sioux Nation of Indians, 448 U.S. 371 (1980)
primarySources.citation: U.S. Supreme Court. (1980). United States v. Sioux Nation of Indians, 448 U.S. 371. Supreme Court of the United States, Washington, D.C. Ruling that the 1877 taking of the Black Hills violated the Fifth Amendment takings clause and awarding $106 million in compensation.

primarySources.title: Office of Indian Affairs Annual Reports to Congress, 1887-1934
primarySources.citation: U.S. Department of the Interior, Office of Indian Affairs. (1887-1934). Annual Reports of the Commissioner of Indian Affairs to the Secretary of the Interior. U.S. Government Publishing Office, Washington, D.C. Documenting land acreage held by Native American tribes across the allotment period.

primarySources.title: U.S. Census Bureau: Native American Population Count, 1900
primarySources.citation: U.S. Census Bureau. (1900). Twelfth Census of the United States: Indian Population. U.S. Government Publishing Office, Washington, D.C. Documenting 237,196 Native Americans in the continental United States.

primarySources.title: Agreement of 1877: Black Hills Cession
primarySources.citation: U.S. Congress. (1877). Agreement with the Sioux Indians, 19 Stat. 254. 44th Congress, Session 2. Transferring the Black Hills from Lakota to federal ownership without the consent of three-fourths of adult male tribal members required by the Treaty of Fort Laramie.
